did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a Computer-Aided Diagnosis (CAD) system for skin cancer classification. The AI methods included pretrained CNN models (VGG16 and ResNet18) for feature extraction, the Genghis Khan Shark Optimizer (GKSO) for feature selection, and K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN) for classification on the ISIC 2016 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a new Computer-Aided Diagnosis (CAD) system using pretrained CNN models (VGG16, ResNet18), the Genghis Khan Shark Optimizer (GKSO) for feature selection, and KNN for classification. It includes a dedicated 'Experiments and Results' section with specific performance metrics (e.g., 93.78% accuracy) on the ISIC 2016 dataset, indicating it is an original technical study rather than a review.

title: Enhanced Skin Cancer Diagnosis Using a CAD System with CNN Feature Extraction and GKSO-Optimized Classification
journal: 2025 XXX International Conference on Information, Communication and Automation Technologies (ICAT)
brief_summary: This study proposes a Computer-Aided Diagnosis (CAD) system for skin cancer detection to address the limitations of traditional, subjective diagnostic methods. The system utilizes pretrained CNN models, specifically VGG16 and ResNet18, for feature extraction from the ISIC 2016 dataset of 1,271 dermoscopic images. To optimize feature selection and reduce dimensionality, the authors employed the Genghis Khan Shark Optimizer (GKSO) alongside a comparative analysis with the Boruta method. The selected features were then classified using K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN), Support Vector Machines (SVM), Logistic Regression, and Random Forest. Experimental results demonstrated that the combination of ResNet18 and GKSO significantly improved performance, with the KNN classifier achieving the highest accuracy of 93.92%. The findings confirm that integrating deep feature extraction with optimization-based feature selection enhances the accuracy and efficiency of skin cancer classification.

lesion_types_studied.details: The ISIC 2016 dataset used in the experiments contains images labeled as malignant. The study focuses on binary classification between malignant and benign cases. Specific malignant types mentioned in the introduction/figure as examples of skin cancer include Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC), Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC), and Melanoma, but the experimental labels are aggregated as 'malignant'.

The ISIC 2016 dataset used in the experiments contains images labeled as benign. The study performs binary classification between malignant and benign cases. The literature review mentions 'common nevi' as part of benign categories in other studies, but the primary experimental labels are 'benign'.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin lesions into malignant or benign categories.
all_task_types.details: The paper performs binary classification to distinguish between malignant and benign skin cancer cases using the ISIC 2016 dataset. The system uses CNNs (VGG16, ResNet18) for feature extraction, GKSO for feature selection, and classifiers (KNN, SVM, LR, RF) for the final prediction. The primary classifier reported is KNN, achieving 93.78% accuracy. The task is explicitly defined as classifying lesions as malignant or benign.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2016
datasets_used.description: A public dataset of dermoscopic images collected by the International Skin Imaging Collaboration, available on Kaggle under the Apache 2.0 License. It includes a variety of skin types, lighting conditions, and lesion sizes.

use.training: Used for training the feature extraction models (VGG16, ResNet18) and the final classifiers (KNN, SVM, LR, RF) after preprocessing and data augmentation.
use.testing: Used for evaluating the performance of the proposed CAD system (e.g., accuracy, sensitivity, specificity).
use.other: Data augmentation was applied to this dataset to generate 2,000 new images to address limited dataset size and enhance model generalization.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To divide the dataset for training the model and evaluating its performance on unseen data.

To identify relevant features by comparing their importance against randomized 'shadow features' using a base classifier (Random Forest) and evaluating feature importance through the Mean Decrease in Accuracy metric.
To optimize feature selection by reducing dimensionality and eliminating irrelevant features, using a fitness value metric for optimization.

To compare the diagnostic accuracy of different feature selection methods (Boruta vs. GKSO) and classifiers (KNN, SVM, LR, RF) using Accuracy, Sensitivity, Specificity, Precision, Recall, and F1 Score.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. It relied on comparative analysis of performance metrics (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, etc.) and stated in the future work section that 'statistical tests' would be conducted in future studies.

ISIC 2016.description: Binary classification of malignant vs. benign skin lesions using an 80:20 train-test split. Data augmentation generated 2,000 additional images.

VGG16 Feature Extraction (No Feature Sel.notes: Performance was suboptimal due to inability to capture fine lesion details and large parameter size.

ResNet18 Feature Extraction (No Feature.notes: Features extracted from Pool5 layer (512-dimensional vectors).

Boruta Feature Selection + Classifiers.notes: Boruta offered moderate improvements but did not surpass performance achieved without feature selection (ResNet18 baseline).

GKSO Feature Selection + KNN.notes: This configuration achieved the highest performance. GKSO population size was 20 with max iterations 100. w1=0.99, w2=0.01.

architecture_used.main_model: Computer-Aided Diagnosis (CAD) system utilizing pretrained CNNs (VGG16 and ResNet18) for feature extraction, Genghis Khan Shark Optimizer (GKSO) for feature selection, and K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN) for classification.
architecture_used.pretraining: VGG16 was initialized with pre-trained ImageNet weights. ResNet18 was used as a pretrained model (specific details of pretraining dataset not explicitly stated beyond standard pretrained status, but implied transfer learning context).

feature_extractor_models.name: VGG16
feature_extractor_models.details: Deep convolutional neural network with 13 convolutional and 3 fully connected layers. Top classification layers removed. Last four layers unfrozen for fine-tuning. Custom classifier head appended: flattening layer, dense layer with 256 ReLU-activated units, dropout layer (rate=0.5), and final sigmoid-activated neuron.

feature_extractor_models.name: ResNet18
feature_extractor_models.details: Lightweight residual network with skip connections. Features extracted from the Pool5 layer, yielding 512-dimensional vectors per image.

feature_selection_algorithm.name: Genghis Khan Shark Optimizer (GKSO)
feature_selection_algorithm.details: Optimization algorithm used to select optimal feature subsets. Population size: 20. Max iterations: 100. Weights: w1=0.99, w2=0.01. Objective function balances error incurred by k-NN and count of selected features.

training_details.data_augmentation: 2,000 new images generated via rotation, flipping, scaling, and contrast enhancement in MATLAB.

preprocessing_techniques: Resizing images to 1024x1024 pixels for consistency and deep learning model compatibility
Grayscale conversion to reduce complexity and highlight texture and shape
BlackHat Filtering to detect dark artifacts like hair by enhancing thin lines
Thresholding to isolate hair from skin using binary masks
Inpainting for hair removal by filling segmented areas with surrounding pixels
Data augmentation including rotation, flipping, scaling, and contrast enhancement to generate 2,000 new images

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied during data augmentation in MATLAB to generate 2,000 new images for variability.

conclusions: The proposed Computer-Aided Diagnosis (CAD) system significantly improves diagnostic accuracy for skin cancer detection by integrating pretrained CNN feature extraction, Genghis Khan Shark Optimizer (GKSO) for feature selection, and K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN) for classification.

ResNet18 proved superior to VGG16 for feature extraction, achieving 87.5% accuracy without feature selection, whereas VGG16 yielded suboptimal results (60-70%) likely due to its inability to capture fine lesion details and large parameter size.

The combination of ResNet18 and GKSO feature selection outperformed the Boruta feature selection method, which only provided marginal improvements and failed to surpass the baseline performance of ResNet18 without feature selection.

The optimal configuration (ResNet18 + GKSO + KNN) achieved an accuracy of 93.78%, sensitivity of 92.35%, and specificity of 94.98% by selecting 260 out of 512 features, demonstrating that optimization-based feature selection enhances classification performance.
The study confirms that combining deep feature extraction with optimization-based feature selection offers a highly accurate and computationally efficient approach for skin cancer classification.
The authors acknowledge limitations regarding the lack of hyperparameter sensitivity analysis and detailed ablation studies, noting these as areas for future work.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the ISIC 2016 dataset (1,271 images) with an 80:20 train-test split, lacking external validation on independent datasets which limits generalizability.

Data augmentation was applied to the training set (generating 2,000 new images) but the paper does not explicitly state if the test set remained untouched, raising potential data leakage concerns if augmentation statistics or parameters were derived from the test set.
The paper acknowledges a lack of hyperparameter sensitivity analysis and detailed ablation studies as a limitation, which weakens the robustness of the reported optimal configuration.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) was performed; significance is inferred solely from comparative performance metrics.
The binary classification task aggregates diverse malignant types (BCC, SCC, Melanoma) into a single 'malignant' class, preventing analysis of model performance across specific cancer subtypes.

The proposed system was evaluated offline using standard metrics (Accuracy, Sensitivity, Specificity) without assessment of clinical deployment feasibility, computational latency, or integration with clinical workflows.
